content: The current I in the circuit shown is _________.
explanation:
Applying KVL in the loop,

$$5 = 2kI + 2k(I + {10^{ - 3}})$$

$$5 = I(4k) + 2 \times {10^3} \times 1 \times {10^{ - 3}}$$

$$I(4k) = 5 - 2$$

$$I = {3 \over {4k}}$$

$$I = 0.75 \times {10^{ - 3}}$$ A

$$I = 0.75$$ mA

content: Consider the circuit shown in the figure. The current I flowing through the 10 $$\Omega$$ resister is ___________.
explanation:
Apply Kirchhoff's circuit laws in loop-1,

$$ \begin{aligned} &2 I_1+3+I_1=0 \\\\ &I_1=-1 \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

$I_1$ can flow in loop -1 only, so current $I$ need to be zero. Otherwise it violets the Kirchhoff's circuit laws.

So, the value of $I=0$.

content:
Consider the circuit shown in the figure.





The current I flowing through the 7$$\Omega$$ resistor between P and Q (rounded off to one decimal place) is ______ A
explanation:
Following is the redrawn and simplified version of the given figure, then we get



In the given circuit resistance,

$$ 3 \Omega \| 6 \Omega=\frac{3 \times 6}{3+6}=\frac{18}{9}=2 \Omega $$ and $2 \Omega \| 2 \Omega=\frac{2 \times 2}{2+2}=\frac{4}{4}=1 \Omega$

Now above circuit reduces as,

By current division rule,

$$ I=5 \times \frac{1}{1+2+7}=\frac{5}{10}=0.5 \mathrm{~A} $$

content: A connection is made consisting of resistance A in series with a parallel combination of resistances B and C. Three resistors of value 10 Ω, 5 Ω, 2 Ω are provided. Consider all possible permutations of the given resistors into the positions A, B, C, and identify the configurations with maximum possible overall resistance, and also the ones with minimum possible overall resistance. The ratio of maximum to minimum values of the resistances (up to second decimal place) is ____________.
explanation:
Considering the figure, we have

$${R_{eq}} = {R_A} + {{{R_B} \times {R_C}} \over {{R_B} + {R_C}}}$$

Req will be maximum when $${R_A} = 10\,\Omega $$

$${R_{eq,\,\max }} = 10 + {{5 \times 2} \over 7} = {{80} \over 7}\,\Omega $$

Req will be minimum when $${R_A} = 2\,\Omega $$

$${R_{eq,\,\min }} = 2 + {{10 \times 5} \over {15}} = {{80} \over {15}}\,\Omega $$

Therefore,

$${{{R_{eq,\,\max }}} \over {{R_{eq,\,\min }}}} = {{15} \over 7} = 2.14$$

content: In the given circuit, the values of V1 and V2 respectively are
explanation:
It is given that current through the 4 Ω resistance in the first branch is I. Therefore, the current through the 4 Ω resistor in second branch is also I

Given circuit is shown below,

Applying KCL at node V1,

$$5 = I + I + 2I$$

$$5 = 4I$$

$$I = {5 \over 4}A$$

Voltage is same for parallel branches

i.e. $${V_1} = 4I$$

$${V_1} = 5$$ Volt

Applying KVL in first loop,

$${V_2} = 5 \times 4 + {V_1}$$

$${V_2} = 20 + 5 = 25$$ Volt

content:
Consider the configuration shown in the figure which is a portion of a larger electrical network

For R = 1 Ω and currents i1 = 2A, i4 = -1A, i5 = -4A, which one of the following is TRUE?

options.content: Data is sufficient to conclude that the supposed currents are impossible
Data is insufficient to identify the current i<sub>2</sub>,i<sub>3</sub> and i<sub>6</sub>.

explanation:
Given, i1 = 2A, i4 = -1A, i5 = -4A

Applying KCL at node (A),

$$-$$i1 $$-$$ i4 + i2 = 0

i2 = i1 + i4

i2 = 2 $$-$$ 1 = 1A

Applying KCL at node (B),

$$-$$i2 $$-$$ i5 + i3 = 0

i3 = i2 + i5

i3 = 1 $$-$$ 4 = $$-$$3 A

Applying KCL at node (C),

i1 $$-$$ i3 $$-$$ i6 = 0

i6 = i1 $$-$$ i3

i6 = 2 $$-$$ ($$-$$3) = 5 A

content: In the figure shown, the value of the current I (in Amperes) is __________.
explanation:
$$ \text { Given circuit is shown below, } $$

Nodal Analysis

Applying KCL at node $V$,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{V-5}{5}+\frac{V}{15}-1=0 \\ & \frac{4 V}{15}=2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V=\frac{15}{2} \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, $I=\frac{V}{15}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~A}=0.5 \mathrm{~A}$

Hence, the value of current $I$ is 0.5 A .

content: Consider a delta connection of resistors and its equivalent star connection as shown below. If all elements of the delta connection are scaled by a factor k, k > 0, the elements of the corresponding star equivalent will be scaled by a factor of
explanation:
Appling star –delta conversion for the given circuit

Before scaling :

$${R_A} = {{{R_b}{R_c}} \over {{R_a} + {R_b} + {R_c}}}$$

If $$R{'_a} = k{R_a}$$, $$R{'_b} = k{R_b}$$, $$R{'_c} = k{R_c}$$

After scaling :

$$R{'_A} = {{R{'_b}R{'_c}} \over {R{'_a} + R{'_b} + R{'_c}}}$$

$$R{'_A} = {{(k{R_b})(k{R_c})} \over {k{R_a} + k{R_b} + k{R_c}}} = {{{k^2}{R_b}{R_c}} \over {k({R_a} + {R_b} + {R_c})}}$$

$$R{'_A} = k{R_A}$$

content: The impedance looking into nodes 1 and 2 in the given circuit is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

Calculation of $R_{T H}$ :

(when dependent sources are present)

(i) Apply a voltage source ' $V_{d c}$ ' between terminal ' 1 ' and ' 2 ' and assume ' $I_{d c}$ ' current flowing to the network.

(ii) Replace all independent source by their internal resistance, i.e. short circuit all the independent voltage source ( $R_{\text {in }}=0$ ) and open circuit all the independent current source $\left(R_{i n}=\infty\right)$.

The Thevenin equivalent impedance is given by,

$$Z_{T H}=\frac{V_{d c}}{I_{d c}}$$

Applying KCL at node $p$,

$$\begin{aligned} & -i_b+\frac{V_{d c}}{100}-99 i_b-I_{d c}=0 \\ & \frac{V_{d c}}{100}-100 i_b=I_{d c}\quad\text{..... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

Applying KVL in the above shown loop,

$$\begin{aligned} & 10 \times 10^3 i_b+V_{d c}=0 \\ & i_b=\frac{-V_{d c}}{10 \times 10^3}\quad\text{..... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From equation (i) and (ii),

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{V_{d c}}{100}-100\left[\frac{-V_{d c}}{10^4}\right]=I_{d c} \\ & \frac{2 V_{d c}}{100}=I_{d c} \\ & \frac{V_{d c}}{I_{d c}}=50 \Omega \\ & Z_{T H}=50 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

content: In the circuit shown below, current through the inductor is
explanation:
According to Kirchhoff's current law at node D, there will be no current in voltage sources.

According to KCL at node $\mathrm{A}$, current through inductor will be

$$ i_1=i+1 $$

Applying Kirchhoff's voltage law in loop ABDC, we have

$$ 1 \times i+(i+1) j 1-1 \angle 0+1 \angle 0=0 $$

Therefore,

$$ i=\frac{-j}{1+j} $$

Therefore from Eqs. (1) and (2), we have

$$ i_1=i+1=\frac{-j}{j+1}+1=\frac{1}{j+1} \mathrm{~A} $$

content: The equivalent inductance measured between the terminals 1 and 2 for the circuit shown in figure, is
explanation:
If current enters the dotted terminals of coil 1 , then a voltage is developed across coil 2 whose higher potential is at dotted terminals. Appling KVL for the given circuit, equation can be given as

$$ \begin{aligned} V &=\frac{-M d i}{d t}+\frac{L_1 d i}{d t}-\frac{M d i}{d t}+L_2 \frac{d i}{d t} \\\\ &=\left(L_1+L_2-2 M\right) \frac{d i}{d t} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ V=L_{\mathrm{eq}} \frac{d i}{d t} $$

where $L_{\mathrm{eq}}$ is the equivalent inductance measured between the terminals 1 and 2 and is given by

$$ L_{\mathrm{cq}}=L_1+L_2-2 M $$

content: The dependent current source shown in Figure
explanation:
Applying KVL in the loop shown in the figure, we have

$$ \begin{aligned} &20-5 I-5\left(I+\frac{V_1}{5}\right)=0 \\\\ &\Rightarrow 20-5 I-5 I-20=0 \\\\ &\Rightarrow 10 I=0 \Rightarrow I=0 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, there is no power delivered by independent source.

For dependent source, we have

$$

Power delivered,

$$ P=I_1^2 R=(4)^2 \times 5=80 \mathrm{~W} $$

content: The Nodal method of circuit analysis is based on
explanation:
The Nodal method of circuit analysis is based on two fundamental principles:

  1. Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL): This law states that the algebraic sum of currents entering a node is zero (or the sum of currents entering equals the sum of currents leaving). In the nodal method, KCL is applied at each independent node to set up equations.

  2. Ohm's Law: This law is used to express the branch currents (i.e., the currents flowing between nodes through resistors or other components) in terms of the unknown node voltages and the known component values. For example, the current through a resistor R connected between node $V_1$ and node $V_2$ is $(V_1 - V_2) / R$.

Therefore, the Nodal method fundamentally uses KCL for its equation formulation and Ohm's law to express the currents in terms of voltages.

Let's evaluate the options:

  • Option A: KVL and Ohm’s law - KVL (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law) is primarily associated with Mesh analysis, not Nodal analysis.

  • Option B: KCL and Ohm’s law - This accurately describes the basis of the Nodal method.

  • Option C: KCL and KVL - While both are fundamental laws, the Nodal method specifically applies KCL to nodes and expresses currents using Ohm's law, rather than directly applying KVL in its formulation.

  • Option D: KCL, KVL and Ohm’s law - As explained, KVL is not directly used in the formulation of nodal equations.

The correct answer is B.

The final answer is $\boxed{B}$

content: Two 2H inductance coils are connected in series and are also magnetically coupled to each other the coefficient of coupling being 0.1.The total inductance of the combination can be
explanation:
Mutual inductance,

$$M = k\sqrt {{L_1}{L_2}} = 0.1\sqrt {2 \times 2} = 0.2$$ H

Total inductance of combination is given by,

$${L_{eq}} = {L_1} + {L_2} \pm 2M$$

$${L_{eq}} = 2 + 2 \pm 0.4 = 4.4$$ H or 3.6 H

Hence, the correct option is (D).

content: A dc circuit shown in figure has a voltage source V, a current source I and several resistors. A particular resistor R dissipates a power of 4 Watts when V alone is active. The same resistor R dissipates a power of 9 Watts when I alone is active. The power dissipated by R when both sources are active will be
explanation:
According to superposition theorem,

I = I1 + I2 (when I1 and I2 are in same direction)

I = I1 $$-$$ I2 (when I1 and I2 are in opposite direction)

Total power is given by,

P = (I1 $$\pm$$ I2)2 R = I$$_1^2$$ R + I$$_2^2$$ R $$\pm$$ 2I1I2 R

P = P1 + P2 $$\pm$$ $$\sqrt {{P_1}{P_2}} $$

P = 4 + 9 $$\pm$$ 12 = 25 W or 1 W

Hence, the correct options are (A) and (D).

content:
Consider the network shown below with R1 = 1 $$\Omega $$, R2 = 2 $$\Omega $$ and R3 = 3 $$\Omega $$. The network is connected to a constant voltage source of 11 V.

The magnitude of the current (in amperes, accurate to two decimal places) through the source is _______.
explanation:
Given R1 = 1 $$\Omega$$, R2 = 2 $$\Omega$$, R3 = 3 $$\Omega$$

Network is symmetric; so VA = VB and VC = VD.

So the circuits reduces to

$${R_{eq}} = ({R_1}||{R_1}) + ({R_1}||{R_1}||{R_3}||{R_3}) + ({R_1}||{R_1})$$

$${R_{eq}} = {{{R_1}} \over 2} + \left( {{{{R_1}} \over 2}||{{{R_3}} \over 2}} \right) + {{{R_1}} \over 2}$$

$$ = {R_1} + {{({R_1} \times {R_3})} \over {{{{R_1}} \over 2} + {{{R_3}} \over 2}}} = {R_1} + {{{R_1}{R_3}} \over {2({R_1} + {R_3})}}$$

$$ = 1 + {{1 \times 3} \over {2(1 + 3)}} = 1 + {3 \over 8} = {{11} \over 8}$$

$${I_s} = {{{V_s}} \over {{R_{eq}}}} = {{11} \over {11/8}} = 8A$$

content: In the circuit shown in the figure, the magnitude of the current (in amperes) through R2 is___________.
explanation:
Nodal equation:

$${{{v_A} - 60} \over 5} - 0.04{v_x} + {{{v_A}} \over 8} = 0$$

Now, $${v_x} = \left( {{{5{v_A}} \over 8}} \right)$$

Therefore, $${{{v_A} - 60} \over 5} - 0.04 \times \left( {{5 \over 8}} \right){v_A} + {{{v_A}} \over 8} = 0$$

0.2 vA $$-$$ 12 $$-$$ 0.025 vA + 0.125 vA = 0

0.3 vA = 12

Therefore, vA = 40 V

Therefore, the current through 3 $$\Omega$$ resistor is

$${{40} \over {3 + 5}}A = 5A$$

modified figure is shown below,

content: In the figure shown, the current i (in ampere) is ______.
explanation:
On applying nodal analysis, we get

$${{{V_1} - 0} \over 1} + {{{V_1} - 8} \over 1} + {{{V_1} - 0} \over 1} + {{{V_1} - 8} \over 1} = 0$$

That is,

$$4{V_1} - 16 = 0$$ Hence, $${V_1} = 4V$$

Therefore,

Current in A branch = $${4 \over 1}A = 4A$$

Current in D branch = $${4 \over 1}A = 4A$$

Current in B branch = $${{8 - 4} \over 1}A = 4A$$

Hence,

Current in C branch = 4 + 1 = 5 A

Again applying nodal analysis, we get

$$i + \left( {{{0 - {V_1}} \over 1}} \right) + 5 = 0$$

Therefore, $$i = {V_1} - 5 = 4 - 5 = - 1A$$

content: In the magnetically coupled circuit shown in the figure, 56 % of the total flux emanating from one coil links the other coil. The value of the mutual inductance (in H) is ______ .

A Y-network has resistances of 10Ω each in two of its arms, while the third arm has a resistance of 11Ω in the equivalent ∆ − network, the lowest value (in Ω) among the three resistances is ______________.
explanation:
$R_a=R_1+R_2+\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_3}$

$R_b=R_1+R_3+\frac{R_1 R_3}{R_2}$

$R_c=R_2+R_3+\frac{R_2 R_3}{R_1}$

Here, $R_a=10+11+\frac{10 \times 11}{10}=32 \Omega$

$R_b=10+11+\frac{10 \times 11}{10}=32 \Omega$

$R_c=10+10+\frac{10 \times 10}{11}=29.09 \Omega$

Hence, the lowest value among the three resistances is $29.09 \Omega$.

content: For the Y-network shown in the figure, the value of R1 (in Ω) in the equivalent ∆ -network is ________.
explanation:
Applying Y to $$\Delta$$ conversion,

$${R_1} = {{{R_a}{R_b} + {R_b}{R_c} + {R_c}{R_a}} \over {{R_b}}}$$

Hence, $${R_1} = {{5 \times 7.5 + 7.5 \times 3 + 3 \times 5} \over {7.5}}$$

R1 = 10$$\Omega$$

Hence, the value of R1 in the equivalent network is 10$$\Omega$$.

content: Three capacitors C1 ,C2 and C3 whose values are 10 µF, 5µF and 2µF respectively, have breakdown voltages of 10V, 5V, and 2V respectively. For the interconnection shown below, the maximum safe voltage in Volts that can be applied across the combination, and the corresponding total charge in µC stored in the effective capacitance across the terminals are respectively.
explanation:
The charge across a capacitor is

$$ Q=C V $$

The breakdown charge of capacitor $C_1$ is

$$ Q_1=C_1 V_1=10 \times 10^{-6} \times 10=100 \mu \mathrm{C} $$

The breakdown charge of capacitor $C_2$ is

$$ Q_2=C_2 V_2=5 \times 10^{-6} \times 5=25 \mu \mathrm{C} $$

The breakdown charge of capacitor $C_3$ is

$$ Q_3=C_3 V_3=2 \times 10^{-6} \times 2=4 \mu \mathrm{C} $$

When the capacitors are in series, the charge is the same. So, the maximum charge on $C_2$ and $C_3$ will be minimum of $\left(Q_2, Q_3\right)=\min \left(25 \mu C, 4 \mu C\right)=4 \mu C=Q_{23}$

Equivalent capacitance of C2 and C3 is,

$${Q_{23}} = {{{C_2}{C_3}} \over {{C_2} + {C_3}}} = {{5 \times 2} \over {5 + 2}} = {{10} \over 7}\mu F$$

Equivalent voltage is,

$${V_{eq}} = {V_{23}} = {{{Q_{23}}} \over {{C_{23}}}} = {{4\mu C} \over {(10/7)\mu F}} = 2.8\,V$$

In parallel, voltage will be same, hence 2.8 V will appear across C1 also.

Charge stored in C1 is given by,

$${Q_1} = {C_1}{V_{eq}}$$

$${Q_1} = 10\mu F \times 2.8\,V = 28\mu C$$

In parallel, total charge is given by,

$${Q_T} = {Q_1} + {Q_{23}}$$

$${Q_T} = (28 + 4)\mu C = 32\mu C$$

content: If VA - VB = 6 V then VC - VD is
explanation:
$$ i=\frac{V_{\mathrm{A}}-V_{\mathrm{B}}}{2}=\frac{6}{2}=3 \mathrm{~A} $$

Applying KCL at node $B$, we have $i=i_2+i_1$ $i_2+i_1=3 \mathrm{~A}$

Applying KCL at node $E$, we have $i_1=i_3+i_4$

Applying KCL at node $D$, we have

$$ i_5=i_2+i_3+i_4=i_2+i_1 $$

Therefore,

$$ i_5=3 \mathrm{~A} $$

Applying KCL at node F, we have

$$ i_6+2+i_5=0 $$

Therefore,

$$ i_6=-2-i_5=-5 \mathrm{~A} $$

Hence,

$$ V_{\mathrm{C}}-V_{\mathrm{D}}=1 \times i_6=-5 \mathrm{~V} $$

content: Impedance Z as shown in the figure is:
explanation:
Applying KVL in given figure,

$$V = I\left[ {j\omega {L_1} + j\omega {L_2} + j\omega {L_3} - j2\omega {M_{13}} + j2\omega {M_{23}}} \right]$$

$$V = I[j5 + j2 + j2 - \underbrace {j2 \times 10}_{Opposite\,coil\,1,3} + \underbrace {j2 \times 10}_{Same\,coil\,2,3}]$$

$$V = (j9)I$$

$$Z = {V \over I} = j9 = j\omega {L_{eq}}$$

Hence, $$Z = j\omega {L_{eq}} = j9$$

content: The rms value of the periodic waveform given in figure is
explanation:
Given circuit is shown below,

$$ i(t)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{-12 t}{T} ; & 0 < t < \frac{T}{2} \\ 6 ; & \frac{T}{2} < t < T \end{array}\right. $$

RMS value is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & I_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T i^2(t) d t} \\ & I_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\left[\int_0^{T / 2}\left(-\frac{12}{T} t\right)^2+\int_{T / 2}^T 6^2 d t\right]} \\ & I_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\left[\int_0^{T / 2}\left(\frac{144}{T^2} t^2\right)+\int_{T / 2}^T 6^2 d t\right]} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & I_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\left[\frac{144}{T^2}\left(\frac{t^3}{3}\right)_0^{T / 2}+36(t)_{T / 2}^T\right]} \\ & I_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\left[\frac{144}{T^2}\left(\frac{T^3}{24}\right)+18 T\right]} \\ & I_{r m s}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{T}[6 T+18 T]}=2 \sqrt{6} \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content: An ideal sawtooth voltage waveform of a frequency 500 Hz and Amplitude 3 V is generated by charging a capacitor of 2 $$\mu F$$ in every cycle the charging requires

options.content: Constant voltage source of 3 V for 1 ms
Constant voltage source of 3 V for 2 ms
Constant current source of 3mA for 1 ms
Constant current source of 3mA for 2 ms

explanation:
Given : $f=500 \mathrm{~Hz}, C=2 \mu \mathrm{~F}$

$$ T=\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{500}=2 \mathrm{msec} $$

Amplitude $=3 \mathrm{~V}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_C(t)=\frac{3}{2 \times 10^{-3}} t=1500 t \\ & \quad[0 < t < 2 \mathrm{msec}] \end{aligned} $$

Current through capacitor is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & I=C \frac{d}{d t} V_C(t) \\ & I=2 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{d}{d t} \times(1500 t) \\ & I=3 \times 10^{-3}=3 \mathrm{~mA} \quad[0 < t < 2 \mathrm{msec}] \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (D).

content: Twelve 1Ω resistances are used as edges to form a cube. The resistance between two diagonally opposite corners of the cube is
explanation:
Equivalent resistant can be calculated as ratio of voltage to current across terminal

$$ \begin{aligned} &V_{a b}=\frac{i}{3} \times 1+\frac{i}{6} \times 1+\frac{i}{3} \times 1 \\\\ &R_{e q}=\frac{V_{a b}}{i}=\frac{5}{6} \Omega \end{aligned} $$

content: For the circuit in Fig. the voltage V0 is

The two electrical sub-network N1 and N2 are connected through three resistors as shown in figure. The voltage across 5 Ω resistor and 1 Ω resistor are given to be 10 V and 5 V, respectively. Then voltage across 15 Ω resistor is
explanation:
Current through 5 $$\Omega $$ resistor,

$${i_2} = {{10} \over 5} = 2A$$,

Current through 1 $$\Omega $$ resistor,

$${i_3} = {5 \over 1} = 5A$$

Applying KCL at N1,

$${i_1} + {i_2} + {i_3} = 0$$

$${i_1} = - ({i_2} + {i_3}) = - 7A$$

Voltage across 15$$\Omega$$ resistor is,

$${V_{15\Omega }} = {i_1} \times 15 = 15 \times ( - 7) = - 105\,V$$

content: A network contains linear resistors and ideal voltage sources. If values of all the resistors are doubled, then the voltage across each resistor is
explanation:
Initially :

Voltage across each resistor,

$${V_R} = V \times {R \over {3R}} = {V \over 3}$$

Finally : R' = 2R

Now voltage across each resistor

$$V{'_R} = V \times {{R'} \over {3R'}}$$

$$V{'_R} = V \times {{2R} \over {6R}}$$

$$V{'_R} = V \times {R \over {3R}} = {V \over 3}$$

If resistors are doubled then current get halved by same factor and voltage across each resistor remains the same.
